content.problemStatement:
You've been working with subarrays for so long that you're sick of them ‚Äď subarrays are now banned!

Formally, consider a set of indices {i1,i2,‚Ä¶,ik}\{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k\}{i1‚Äč,i2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ik‚Äč} such that 1‚Č§i1<i2<‚čĮ<ik‚Č§N1 \leq i_1 \lt i_2 \lt \cdots \lt i_k \leq N1‚Č§i1‚Äč<i2‚Äč<‚čĮ<ik‚Äč‚Č§N.

This set is said to be an anti-subarray if ik‚ąíi1>k‚ąí1i_k - i_1 \gt k - 1ik‚Äč‚ąíi1‚Äč>k‚ąí1.

In particular, note that an anti-subarray must have length greater than 111.

You have an array AAA containing NNN (N‚Č•4N \geq 4N‚Č•4) integers, a boolean array BBB of length NNN, and an integer XXX.

You can perform the following operation:


Choose an index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N) such that Bi=1B_i = 1Bi‚Äč=1.
Then, increase AiA_iAi‚Äč by 111.
Find the minimum number of operations needed to obtain an anti-subarray whose sum is **at least** XXX.

It is guaranteed that BBB contains at least one element equal to 111.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX ‚Äď the length of the array and the target sum.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1‚Äč,B2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a single integer: the minimum number of operations needed to obtain an anti-subset of AAA with sum ‚Č•X\geq X‚Č•X.
constraints: Bi‚ąą{0,1}B_i \in \{0, 1\}Bi‚Äč‚ąą{0,1}, and at least one element of BBB equals 111.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We're allowed to increase any element. One optimal solution is:

Increase A2A_2A2‚Äč by 111, to obtain [‚ąí200,6,100,2][-200, 6, 100, 2][‚ąí200,6,100,2].
Increase A4A_4A4‚Äč by 111, to obtain [‚ąí200,6,100,3][-200, 6, 100, 3][‚ąí200,6,100,3].
Now there's an anti-subarray with sum ‚Č•9\geq 9‚Č•9, formed by indices {2,4}\{2, 4\}{2,4}.

Test case 222: Same array and target as the previous test, but now only A3A_3A3‚Äč can be increased.
Increasing it 107107107 times results in [‚ąí200,5,207,2][-200, 5, 207, 2][‚ąí200,5,207,2], which has the anti-subarray with indices {1,3,4}\{1, 3, 4\}{1,3,4} giving a sum of ‚ąí200+207+2=9-200 + 207 + 2 = 9‚ąí200+207+2=9, which is the best we can do.
Test case 444: There's already an anti-subarray with sum ‚Č•X=70\geq X = 70‚Č•X=70, so no operations are needed.
